# Literature Review

This chapter develops the theoretical and empirical foundation for the proposed framework. It achieves this objective through a critical review of four interconnected bodies of literature: the conceptual foundations of technical debt, existing measurement and prioritisation approaches, technical debt governance in financial and regulated enterprise IT, and multi criteria decision-making. Together, these strands support a synthesis that identifies the research gap and motivates the research questions addressed in this dissertation.

## Context of the Topic

Technical debt has become both a central concept and a persistent management problem in software engineering and enterprise IT governance [@avgeriouTechnicalDebtManagement2024]. Ward Cunningham first introduced the metaphor in 1992, describing technical debt as the long term cost of short term technical compromises [@cunninghamWyCashPortfolioManagement1992]. Over time, the concept has expanded from a focus on poor code and shortcuts in implementation to a broader understanding that also includes architectural, infrastructure, organisational and process aspects of complex IT systems [@kruchtenTechnicalDebtMetaphor2012].

Despite this conceptual expansion, much of the empirical and tool supported literature remains anchored at the source code level. It relies heavily on static analysis tools and code based metrics. These approaches work tolerably well for greenfield development dominated by in house code. They are much less appropriate for enterprise environments where operational reliability depends on vendor platforms, legacy infrastructure, and tightly integrated service stacks rather than solely on internally developed software [@amanatidisEvaluatingAgreementTechnical2020].

The present review is motivated by a gap at the intersection of technical debt management, multi criteria decision-making, and mission-critical enterprise IT operations. Existing frameworks do not provide a quantitative, service level mechanism for measuring and prioritising technical debt in environments such as trading IT. In these contexts, the most consequential forms of debt manifest not in code repositories but in operational outcomes such as incident frequency, recovery time, patch delay, and change failure rate [@ramasubbuControllingTechnicalDebt2021]. The following sections map the state of knowledge across these three domains and build a theoretical and methodological basis for the proposed framework.

## Perspective and Framework

The review adopts a critical, problem oriented perspective rather than an exhaustive cataloguing of all work on technical debt. Its structure is intended to build a coherent argument: that existing measurement and prioritisation approaches are not adequate for service level, enterprise IT contexts, and that a quantitative framework applying TOPSIS to service management data represents a defensible and novel contribution.

To support this argument, the review is organised into four thematic areas that align with the four pillars of the research design. The first examines the conceptual foundations of technical debt. The second surveys current measurement and prioritisation frameworks and their limitations. The third focuses on technical debt in financial and regulated enterprise IT. The fourth introduces multi criteria decision-making and makes the case for TOPSIS as an appropriate method in this setting.

## Methodology for Literature Selection

The literature search was conducted between January and March 2026 using a systematic and reproducible approach. It drew on four principal academic databases: the University of Essex library, Scopus, IEEE Xplore and Google Scholar. Search strings were tailored to each thematic area to capture both foundational and current work.

| Theme | Search String |
|---|---|
| TD foundations | `"technical debt" AND (metaphor OR taxonomy OR typology OR theory)` |
| TD measurement and tools | `"technical debt" AND (measurement OR "static analysis" OR SonarQube OR metric OR "code smell")` |
| TD prioritisation | `"technical debt" AND (priorit* OR "backlog management" OR remediation OR repayment)` |
| TD in enterprise and financial IT | `"technical debt" AND ("enterprise" OR "financial services" OR "trading" OR "legacy system" OR "regulated")` |
| TD automation | `"technical debt management" AND ("machine learning" OR intelligent OR automation OR NLP)` |
| MCDM and TOPSIS | `TOPSIS AND ("multi-criteria" OR MCDM OR "decision making" OR priorit*)` |
| Patch management | `"patch management" AND (priorit* OR risk OR vulnerability OR SLA OR enterprise)` |
| Operational risk and DORA | `("DORA metrics" OR "mean time to restore") AND ("technical debt" OR "software delivery")` |
: Search terms used for literature review {#tbl-status tbl-colwidths="[30,70]"}

The selection focused primarily on peer-reviewed journal articles, conference proceedings and empirical technical reports from the last six years. Exceptions were made for topics with no recent resarch and for foundational works that define key concepts or methods, such as @cunninghamWyCashPortfolioManagement1992 and @hwangMultipleAttributeDecision1981. All selected sources were available in full text in English and were directly relevant to at least one thematic pillar.

Opinion pieces without empirical grounding, studies confined to agile process improvement without a measurable technical debt component, and duplicates superseded by more comprehensive journal versions were excluded. Forward and backward citation snowballing was applied to four anchor publications: @kruchtenTechnicalDebtMetaphor2012, @lenarduzziSystematicLiteratureReview2021 and @forsgrenAccelerate2018. The search yielded approximately 140 candidate sources, of which more than 60 were retained following full text screening.

## Analytical Approach

The review uses narrative synthesis rather than a formal systematic review protocol. Two reasons support this choice. First, the research spans three disciplines. These include software engineering, systems and enterprise IT governance, and decision science. These fields differ significantly in their research methods, terminology and evidence standards. A single meta analytic framework would not accommodate this heterogeneity. Second, the aim of the review is to build a coherent argument in support of a novel design science artefact. In this context, narrative synthesis is more appropriate than statistical aggregation [@hevnerDesignScienceInformation2004].

Each thematic section follows the same pattern. It begins by synthesising convergent findings. It then identifies tensions, contradictions and gaps. Finally, it positions these gaps in relation to design decisions for the proposed framework. In this way, the review remains tightly coupled to the subsequent research design.

## Conceptual Foundations of Technical Debt

The first thematic area traces the evolution of the technical debt concept. It moves from the original metaphor to later theoretical developments and then to the expansion of the concept beyond software into systems engineering, infrastructure and vendor platforms. Understanding this evolution is essential because most existing tools and practices are still tuned to an earlier, narrower view of technical debt and so misalign with the realities of contemporary enterprise IT.

### Origins and the Metaphor

@cunninghamWyCashPortfolioManagement1992 introduced the metaphor of technical debt to explain his refactoring strategy to non-technical stakeholders. He compared shipping “not quite right” code to taking on financial debt. The development team gains speed and delivers earlier, but every additional maintenance effort on that code represents interest. If the principal is not repaid through refactoring, the cost of working with the system grows until further change becomes very difficult or impossible.

Several influential thinkers refined the concept. @mcconnellManagingTechnicalDebt2008 distinguished between unintentional debt, arising from poor practice, and intentional debt, incurred as a conscious trade-off, and introduced a cost structure of principal, recurring interest, and compounding interest. @fowlerTechnicalDebtQuadrant2009 added a quadrant distinguishing reckless from prudent debt and deliberate from inadvertent debt, arguing that even the best teams inevitably accumulate some inadvertent debt as their understanding of the problem matures. @gatTechnicalDebt2010 cautioned that when debt accumulates to the point where it equals or exceeds the value of the system itself, the code becomes "toxic" and further investment yields negative returns. @kruchtenTechnicalDebtMetaphor2012 later formalised these ideas into a taxonomy covering code, design, architectural, test, build and documentation debt.

### Theoretical Development

Subsequent empirical studies have confirmed and extended this view. @beskerTechnicalDebtEmpirical reported that developers in industrial settings spend roughly 23% of their time dealing with the consequences of existing technical debt, with architectural debt exerting the strongest negative influence on productivity. @bediUnderstandingFactorsAffecting2022 used regression analysis on open source repositories and found that commit frequency, lines of code, code smells, test coverage are all significant predictors of technical debt, whereas developer reputation metrics are not. Their work suggests that technical debt is driven primarily by development practices rather than by the perceived quality of individual developers.

@ahmadTechnicalDebtNot2026 approached the topic qualitatively, interviewing experts from four international companies. They identified five non-technical dimensions of debt accumulation in large scale agile environments: social dynamics, process, people, documentation and requirements. Across these dimensions, a lack of communication and collaboration emerged as the dominant driver. This reinforces the view of technical debt as a sociotechnical phenomenon.

@vidoniInfiniteTechnicalDebt2022 advanced a more radical theoretical argument through a game theoretic lens. They argued that in complex systems technical debt cannot be completely removed and that its effects cannot be considered fully controlled, even when individual items are repaid. The concept of “infinite technical debt” challenges the assumption, implicit in many repayment focused frameworks, that debt is always recoverable given sufficient effort. It also raises an ethical concern: if a system has passed into a state where meaningful repayment is no longer feasible, continuing to emphasise local repayments may offer an illusion of governance while delaying necessary replacement decisions.

### Expanding the Conceptual Perimeter

The conceptual perimeter of technical debt has progressively widened to include broader systems and lifecycle concerns. @kleinwaksTechnicalDebtSystems2023 conducted a systematic review of technical debt in systems engineering and found that requirements, architecture, integration artefacts and verification artefacts can all accumulate debt. They also concluded that many software oriented technical debt approaches are poorly adapted to multidisciplinary systems engineering lifecycles. @hanssenIdentifyingScalabilityDebt2019 identified scalability debt as a distinct subtype of technical debt triggered by regulatory change. In an open banking case study they showed that architectural decisions that were sound before the introduction of the EU Payment Services Directive 2 (PSD2) became sources of debt once new scalability requirements were imposed without revisiting the architecture. This illustrates how external regulatory change can convert previously adequate designs into liabilities. @yangTaxonomyTechnicalDebt2019 proposed a taxonomy of Commercial Off-the-Shelf (COTS) related technical debt that covers seven key types of debt arising during system acquisition and integration. These include vendor platform obsolescence and integration constraints. Managing such forms of debt requires different tools from those used for internally developed code. For financial trading IT, where vendor platforms and COTS products are central, this broader view is particularly relevant. Many of the most consequential debts in this domain—platform debt, infrastructure debt and vulnerability debt—are not detectable by standard static analysis.

## Measurement and Prioritisation Frameworks

The second thematic area examines how technical debt is measured and prioritised in practice. It argues that current measurement is heavily concentrated at the code level and that existing prioritisation frameworks lack the multidimensional and quantitative rigour required for robust governance in enterprise settings.

### The Dominance of Static Analysis and Its Limits

Static code analysis tools such as SonarQube, SQALE and CAST dominate current practice. They typically operationalise technical debt as a weighted sum of estimated remediation effort. The estimates are derived from rule violations detected by automated scanners [@biazottoTechnicalDebtManagement2024].
In a systematic mapping study, @biazottoTechnicalDebtManagement2024 identified 178 primary studies and 121 automation artefacts for technical debt management. They found that most artefacts operate at the code level, that many still require substantial human intervention even at higher automation levels, and that end to end automation across the technical debt management lifecycle is rare. The focus on code leaves infrastructure level and platform level debt largely unaddressed. @ampatzoglouSDK4EDPlatformTechnical2022 presented SDK4ED as a comprehensive industry oriented platform integrating several code level techniques. A three-year evaluation suggested that SDK4ED can deliver financial benefits to software organisations. At the same time, the platform offers only limited support for debt related to infrastructure or vendor platforms.
@tornhillCodeRedBusiness2022 analysed 39 proprietary production codebases drawn from multiple industries. They found that poor code quality is statistically associated with higher defect density, longer remediation cycles and reduced developer throughput. This confirms that code level debt has clear business consequences. It does not, however, change the fact that an exclusive focus on code creates blind spots. Organisations may allocate significant resources to “safe” refactoring while leaving critical legacy infrastructure and unsupported components unaddressed.
@amanatidisEvaluatingAgreementTechnical2020 evaluated the agreement between different technical debt assessment tools applied to 50 open source projects. They observed substantial divergence: tools that assess the same codebase produce different debt estimates and highlight different violation sets. No single tool consistently dominated. This implies that a debt figure generated by one tool is not an objective measurement but a function of that tool’s ruleset and thresholds. @albuquerqueManagingTechnicalDebt2023 reviewed 150 primary studies on intelligent techniques for technical debt management. They found that most approaches remain at low to intermediate levels of automation and that human involvement is still required at several stages. They also noted that infrastructure debt and process debt remain significantly understudied compared to code and architectural debt.

### The Prioritisation Gap

@lenarduzziSystematicLiteratureReview2021 carried out the most comprehensive review of technical debt prioritisation strategies to date. They analysed 44 primary studies and found that existing approaches cluster around cost based, value based and risk based strategies. Most of these approaches adopt a single primary criterion. Quantitative methods are less common than qualitative or heuristic techniques, and empirical validation on industrial datasets is rare. The authors concluded that the field lacks agreement on which factors matter, how to measure them and what constitutes an adequate prioritisation process. This conclusion directly motivates the quantitative prioritisation contribution of the present work. Concrete evidence demonstrates the cost of inadequate prioritisation. @detoledoReducingIncidentsMicroservices2021 showed in a production microservices system that targeted repayment of architectural technical debt items led to an 84% reduction in incidents. This result anchors the abstract case for technical debt management in a measurable operational outcome and suggests incident reduction as a meaningful evaluation criterion for prioritisation frameworks.

Empirical studies also reveal a clear gap between tool generated priorities and practitioner behaviour. @alfayezHowSonarQubeidentifiedTechnical2023 studied 89 practitioners from four companies. They found that most practitioners considered both value and cost when deciding which items to repay, rather than following a single strategy. They also reported that perceived value was highly context dependent and subjective, which challenges the assumption that any single ranking rule can capture all relevant considerations. @limBalancingActWhat2012 interviewed practitioners across several organisations. They found that repayment decisions are strongly shaped by organisational pressures, interpersonal dynamics and competing business priorities. These factors are difficult to encode in algorithms. The implication is that quantitative prioritisation tools should be seen as decision support rather than decision replacement. From a managerial perspective, @wieseITManagersPerspective2023 interviewed 16 IT managers and found, that most did not have a formal technical debt management process in place. The primary challenge they identified was communicating about technical debt across technical and business stakeholders. @stochelBusinessdrivenTechnicalDebt2023 proposed the Continuous Debt Valuation Approach (CoDVA), which assigns a financial value to each debt item based on remediation cost, interest rate and business risk exposure. Case studies show that this valuation can significantly change repayment priorities compared with effort only approaches. However, CoDVA still relies on development level data and does not incorporate signals from operational service management, such as incident rates or change failure rates.

These studies also highlight an ethical dimension. Any prioritisation framework embeds value judgements about which systems, users and risks matter most. In financial institutions, priority scores may influence funding, staffing and post incident accountability. Where weighting schemes and decision rules are opaque, they may conceal these value choices under a veneer of technical objectivity.

## Technical Debt in Financial and Regulated Enterprise IT

The third thematic area applies the general insights from the technical debt literature to the specific setting of financial services and regulated trading IT. It focuses on how debt accumulates in these environments, how it translates into operational harm, and how related work in security and patch management quantifies this harm.

### The Financial Services Context

Financial services organisations are characterised by complex, layered legacies, strict regulatory compliance requirements and stringent service availability obligations. Architectures often depend on vendor platforms and COTS products that cannot be freely refactored [@hakiDigitalNudgingTechnical2023].

@hakiDigitalNudgingTechnical2023 conducted clinical research at Credit Suisse, a former global financial services institution based in Zurich. They designed and introduced a digital nudge for technical debt management across more than 3,000 IT applications. As part of this work, they defined a technical debt taxonomy with four main categories: code quality debt, infrastructure lifecycle debt, vulnerability debt and automation debt. Infrastructure lifecycle debt was measured as cumulative months of unsupported components. Vulnerability debt was derived from weighted counts of vulnerabilities detected by network scanners. Automation debt reflected build and deployment maturity. This taxonomy is important because it shows that, at scale, a major financial institution assigns at least as much importance to infrastructure and vulnerability debt as to code level debt.

The empirical basis of the evaluation is, however, limited. The quantitative analysis focused on three development teams. Two teams reduced their debt index over one year and one did not. The authors identified management intent as the decisive factor, rather than the design of the nudge itself. Furthermore, the use of a clinical research design, with researchers embedded in the organisation, raises questions about independence. These factors do not invalidate the conceptual contribution, but they suggest caution in generalising from the observed outcomes.

Rolland and Lyytinen investigated the organisational dynamics of architectural debt accumulation in a large Scandinavian financial institution they called BankAlpha [@rollandExploringTensionsManagement2021]. They conducted eleven in-depth interviews with business managers, enterprise architects, IT architects and software developers, supplemented by meeting notes and archival documents. Their analysis applied Carlile's knowledge boundary framework to explain why architectural debt becomes invisible across distributed agile teams. They identified two persistent tensions. The first is between decentralised feature delivery teams, which optimise for local throughput, and the architectural governance required to manage debt at the system level. The second is between the pace of digital innovation and the long-term sustainability of the architecture that must support it. Both tensions are intensified in financial services by compliance obligations and competitive pressure to release new products rapidly. The study is notable for grounding these dynamics in empirical data from a regulated banking institution. Its primary limitation is the single-case design. Whether the patterns observed at BankAlpha generalise to other institutions remains an open question. Nevertheless, the paper offers a theoretically grounded account of why architectural debt accumulates even in organisations that acknowledge its importance, and why governance instruments that rely solely on technical measurement are unlikely to resolve the underlying organisational conflicts.

### Enterprise Systems and the Outsourcing Context

@bankerTechnicalDebtFirm2020 examined 26 firms operating a commercial COTS CRM system over its full eleven-year lifecycle. Using propensity score matching against firms with zero technical debt, they found that a 10% increase in technical debt reduced gross return on assets by 16% on average. Critically, this penalty more than doubled within six years of adoption as code decay compounded structural complexity. While all firms initially benefited from system customisation, those carrying median debt levels saw the net value of their system turn negative by year five, a pattern robust to alternative performance measures including asset turnover, gross margin, and operating income.

In a later study, they extended this analysis to 1,824 outsourced remediation projects involving 424 client organisations [@ramasubbuControllingTechnicalDebt2021]. They found that “control balancing” between client and vendor improved remediation outcomes only when both parties had agreed a long term migration roadmap. In the absence of such a roadmap, increased coordination actually harmed performance. For prioritisation frameworks, this implies that ranking debt items without embedding them in a strategic roadmap can be counterproductive.

The study “Getting Trapped in Technical Debt” provides a complementary perspective [@rinta-kahilaGettingTrappedTechnical2023]. In an explanatory case study of a global manufacturing firm’s COTS implementation, the authors showed how staged legacy replacement, combined with recurrent customisations to restore local flexibility, led to self reinforcing architectural debt accumulation. Over time, the system moved into a debt constrained state in which further modifications became overly difficult. A system dynamics model captured reinforcing feedback loops between customisation decisions, debt accumulation and declining digital options. This work suggests that organisations often do not consciously choose to neglect technical debt. Instead, they are trapped by interactions between technical design choices and organisational routines. Standard code level tools are not capable of revealing such dynamics. Governance level visibility of infrastructure and architectural debt is required to detect and address them before they become irreversible.

### DORA Metrics as Operational Debt Proxies

@forsgrenAccelerate2018 developed and validated the DORA metrics across more than 2,000 organisations. They showed that four indicators, deployment frequency, lead time for changes, mean time to restore service, and change failure rate, distinguish high performing from low performing delivery teams. A crucial feature of these metrics is that they can be derived from operational systems such as CI/CD pipelines and IT service management tools without access to source code.

In contrast, @paudelExploringRelationshipTechnical2025 investigated the relationship between technical debt density and lead time across six components at a large fintech company. The results were mixed. Two components showed a moderate positive correlation, two showed a moderate negative correlation, and two showed no meaningful relationship. Technical debt density explained between five and forty-one percent of lead time variance depending on the component. The authors concluded that technical debt alone cannot explain lead time and that other variables, including change size, component ownership and team structure, are likely confounding factors. This result tempers claims about the operational impact of code-level debt and reinforces the case for multi-criteria measurement rather than reliance on a single proxy.

Using operational service management data as a basis for technical debt prioritisation raises important ethical questions regardless of the specific metric used. Such records are generated and owned by operations teams. Development teams may not have consented to their use in performance evaluations. A framework that ranks services based on operational indicators may influence budgets, staffing and reputational assessments. Any use of such data therefore requires explicit data governance principles and transparency about how metrics are attributed, aggregated and interpreted.

### Security Debt and Patch Management

Security related technical debt is most visible in the literature on patch management and vulnerability handling. @tizioSoftwareUpdatesStrategies2023 analysed a dataset of 86 Advanced Persistent Threats (APT) and 350 campaigns dated between 2008 and 2020 to quantify the risk of different software update strategies. Contrary to common assumption, most APT campaigns exploited publicly known vulnerabilities rather than zero-days. Enterprises delaying updates by one month faced 4.9 times higher odds of compromise compared to immediate patching, rising to 9.1 times at three months.

@markkanenPatchManagementPlanning2023 reviewed the academic literature and current NIST guidance on patch management planning to identify gaps and propose enriched recommendations for enterprise organisations. They argue that uniform, time-based patching policies are increasingly inadequate given the shrinking window between vulnerability disclosure and exploitation, and that organisations should instead treat patching as a risk-based, organisation-wide business priority rather than a purely technical responsibility. @nursePatchNotPatch2025 framed patch prioritisation as a problem of maintaining service-level agreement (SLA) compliance, noting that business partners and clients drive patching decisions through supply chain agreements and contractual requirements. This aligns patch management with the service level focus of the present research.

@kulaReleasingFastSlow2019 provided further evidence from ING, where they studied release practices and dependency management. They showed that keeping upstream dependencies current is a persistent challenge in rapid release banking environments and that delays in dependency updates contribute to technical debt. This finding is consistent with the broader argument, supported by Forsgren, Humble and Kim as well as Paudel *et al.*, that technical debt manifests in ways that are detectable through operational service management data [@forsgrenAccelerate2018; @paudelExploringRelationshipTechnical2025]

## Multi-Criteria Decision-Making and TOPSIS

The fourth thematic area turns to decision science. The previous sections established that technical debt in enterprise IT is multidimensional, that current tools largely ignore the most important dimensions in enterprise settings, and that no validated quantitative prioritisation framework exists. This section argues that multi-criteria decision-making (MCDM) methods are well suited to the prioritisation problem and that TOPSIS, in particular, is an appropriate method for the proposed framework.

### The Case for Multi-Criteria Methods

Technical debt prioritisation involves balancing several criteria at once. These include remediation cost, business value, security risk, operational impact and regulatory compliance. Existing practice and empirical studies show that practitioners weigh these dimensions together rather than focusing on a single factor [@alfayezHowSonarQubeidentifiedTechnical2023]. Collapsing them into a single ad-hoc indicator risks discarding information that matters in real decisions. @lenarduzziSystematicLiteratureReview2021 concluded that the absence of a validated, quantitative, multi criteria framework is a central obstacle to evidence based technical debt governance. MCDM methods provide formal mechanisms for integrating multiple, incommensurable criteria. Among these methods, TOPSIS is attractive because of its intuitive interpretation and computational simplicity.

@hwangMultipleAttributeDecision1981 introduced TOPSIS, defining the ideal solution as the hypothetical alternative scoring best on all criteria simultaneously and ranking alternatives by Euclidean proximity to this ideal. @zanakisMultiattributeDecisionMaking1998 compared several MCDM methods and found that TOPSIS tends to produce rankings that are stable and close to the Pareto optimal ordering across a range of problem structures. This supports its use in prioritisation tasks under uncertainty.

### TOPSIS: Mechanics, Applications, and Critical Limitations
TOPSIS operates by constructing a normalised weighted decision matrix, identifying ideal best and worst values per criterion, computing each alternative's Euclidean distance to both reference points, and ranking by the closeness coefficient, defined as the ratio of the distance to the ideal worst to the sum of both distances [@albarakManagingTechnicalDebt2022]. A well known issue with TOPSIS is rank reversal: the addition or removal of alternatives can change the ranking of existing options [@garcia-cascalesRankReversalTOPSIS2012]. @yangIngeniousSolutionRank2020 proposed an adjustment to the normalisation process to mitigate rank reversal by defining reference points independently of the specific set of alternatives. This is particularly relevant for enterprise portfolios where services are regularly added, modified or decommissioned.

@shekhovtsovComparativeCaseStudy2020 compared TOPSIS and VIKOR across several decision problems. They found that the two methods produce similar rankings when criterion weights are well specified, but diverge when weights are uncertain or criteria conflict strongly. They suggested that TOPSIS is preferable when the objective is a global ranking of alternatives across all criteria, whereas VIKOR is better suited to identifying compromise solutions between conflicting criteria. Because the present research aims to generate a ranked list of services for remediation, rather than to identify compromise solutions, TOPSIS appears more appropriate.

Several studies demonstrate that TOPSIS can be applied in technical and risk intensive contexts. @albarakManagingTechnicalDebt2022 and colleagues combined TOPSIS with portfolio theory to prioritise database normalisation debt. Their results showed that TOPSIS highlighted high priority debt items that would likely have been deferred under effort only approaches. @khanIntegratedMethodologyRanking2022 applied a hybrid PROMETHEE CRITIC and TOPSIS CRITIC approach to web service ranking, using CRITIC to derive objective weights. @zytoonDecisionSupportModel2020 used a Delphi–AHP–TOPSIS combination to prioritise safety inspections in a regulated industrial environment. @ishakAnalysisFuzzyAHPTOPSIS2020 applied fuzzy AHP–TOPSIS to improve robustness when weights cannot be specified with precision. All of these studies provide precedents for using TOPSIS to support multi criteria decisions under risk and regulation.

## Transferability Critique and Ethical Dimensions

Although these applications offer relevant precedents, none of them operates on live service management data in large enterprise settings. Most rely on researcher defined scenarios, synthetic datasets or controlled development artefacts. The proposed research differs by applying TOPSIS to operational records such as incident logs, ITSM change data and vulnerability scan outputs. These data sources are known to have quality issues. Incident classifications can vary between teams. Recorded change failure rates can be influenced by documentation practices as much as by technical quality. Vulnerability counts depend on scanner coverage and configuration.

@grecoMethodologicalFrameworkComposite2019 examined composite indices based on MCDM methods and emphasised that their robustness depends strongly on input data quality. They argued that sensitivity analysis with respect to criterion weights is a necessary component of any such index. For the proposed framework, this implies that data limitations must be explicitly acknowledged, that the sensitivity of rankings to weight changes must be studied, and that outputs must be interpreted with appropriate caution.

The ethical dimension of criterion weighting is equally important. Weights are not neutral technical parameters but normative choices about organisational priorities. A framework that places a high weight on vulnerability debt relative to infrastructure lifecycle debt expresses a judgement that security exposure is more important than supportability. In a regulated financial institution, such choices have governance and possibly legal implications [@grecoMethodologicalFrameworkComposite2019]. The framework must therefore include a transparent and auditable process for weight elicitation. This process should consider aspects from operations, security, risk and business leadership perspectives, and the resulting weights and their rationale should be documented and visible in all governance outputs.

## Synthesis: Converging Evidence and the Research Gap

The four thematic sections together define the conceptual, methodological, contextual and technical landscape within which this research is situated. This synthesis draws out the main points of convergence across the literature, acknowledges important limitations and articulates the research gap that the proposed framework addresses.

### Cross Pillar Convergence

Four converging findings are particularly significant. First, the concept of technical debt has expanded to encompass all layers of the enterprise IT stack, but measurement practice has not kept pace. The dominant tools still focus on code level debt, leaving infrastructure lifecycle debt, platform obsolescence and other critical forms of enterprise debt largely unmeasured [@kruchtenTechnicalDebtMetaphor2012; @yangTaxonomyTechnicalDebt2019; @biazottoTechnicalDebtManagement2024].

Second, technical debt prioritisation is both empirically and theoretically a multi criteria problem. No widely accepted quantitative framework currently exists that can integrate cost, value, risk and operational impact into a single, auditable ranking [@lenarduzziSystematicLiteratureReview2021]. Practitioner studies confirm that both developers and managers routinely consider multiple factors when making repayment decisions [@alfayezHowSonarQubeidentifiedTechnical2023; @pinaTechnicalDebtPrioritization2022].

Third, the consequences of unmanaged technical debt are both measurable and substantial. Targeted repayment of architectural debt can dramatically reduce incidents [@detoledoReducingIncidentsMicroservices2021]. The financial harm of technical debt compounds progressively over a system's lifecycle [@bankerTechnicalDebtFirm2020]. Repeated customisation of COTS systems can drive organisations into debt-constrained states [@rinta-kahilaGettingTrappedTechnical2023]. Poor code quality reduces throughput and increases defects [@tornhillCodeRedBusiness2022]. Delayed patching increases vulnerability to exploitation [@feutrillEffectCommonVulnerability2018]. Together these studies show that technical debt produces quantifiable, progressive operational harm that is visible in service level data before it becomes evident in code.

Fourth, DORA metrics and related operational indicators offer a viable bridge between technical debt and observable service level performance. @forsgrenAccelerate2018 showed that these metrics capture aspects of technical health even when source code is inaccessible. @paudelExploringRelationshipTechnical2025 provided further evidence by linking technical debt accumulation directly to degradation in lead time. This suggests that service management data can serve as a legitimate measurement basis for technical debt in vendor dominated trading IT environments.

### Cross Cutting Limitations

At the same time, several limitations qualify these conclusions. The empirical base for financial IT and trading systems remains relatively thin. Studies such as Haki *et al.’s* work at Credit Suisse and *Kula et al.’s* analysis at ING provide valuable insights but are restricted to single organisations [@hakiDigitalNudgingTechnical2023; @kulaReleasingFastSlow2019].

Existing applications of TOPSIS and other MCDM methods to technical debt rely on controlled datasets rather than live operational records [@albarakManagingTechnicalDebt2022; @khanIntegratedMethodologyRanking2022]. The comparative work of @salabunComparativeCaseStudy2020 shows that method choice matters less than weight choice when weights are uncertain, which underscores the importance of careful and transparent weight determination.

Behavioural studies emphasise that management intent, incentives and communication patterns often exert a stronger influence on remediation outcomes than technical tools do [@wieseITManagersPerspective2023; @beskerTechnicalDebtEmpirical; @hakiDigitalNudgingTechnical2023]. Technical frameworks can support, but not replace, governance and leadership.

Finally, the ethical dimensions of operating on service level operational data, assigning criterion weights and attributing responsibility for technical debt have received little explicit attention in the technical debt literature. The present work responds to this gap by embedding transparency and governance considerations into the design and evaluation of the proposed framework.

### Statement of the Research Gap

No existing framework integrates service level operational metrics, multi criteria prioritisation and the specific constraints of financial trading IT into a single, auditable technical debt ranking instrument. Code centric tools are structurally inapplicable to many vendor hosted and regulated platforms. Single criterion models conflict with both the empirical evidence on practitioner behaviour and the theoretical structure of the prioritisation problem. Qualitative governance frameworks lack the quantitative precision and repeatability required by risk and compliance functions in regulated institutions.

Moreover, TOPSIS has not been applied to service level technical debt data in an enterprise setting. The financial trading IT domain is almost absent from empirical technical debt research, despite being one of the environments where the risks associated with unmanaged technical debt are most severe.

### Transition to Research Questions

These observations motivate two research questions. The first concerns measurement: which operational service management metrics can act as valid proxies for technical debt severity in enterprise trading IT systems where source code analysis is not feasible, and how can these metrics be combined into a replicable and auditable multi criteria framework? The second concerns evaluation: does a TOPSIS based ranking of services, constructed from such metrics, align more closely with actual operational risk exposure than existing practitioner heuristics, and is that alignment sufficient for the framework to function as a defensible governance instrument in a regulated financial institution?

Taken together, these questions position the proposed framework at the convergence of the four reviewed pillars and set the stage for the design science work presented in the subsequent chapters.
